Swiss CitiesGruyères

Gruyères

FRFribourg

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The crane who named a kingdom

Retelling of the founding myth: a child follows a silver crane across the crimson evening sky to the hilltop where Gruyères will be born. The crane whispers the town's name and becomes its heraldic symbol forever — a tale of how a single bird's flight shaped the destiny of a medieval kingdom in the Pre-Alps.

Create This Story

According to legend, Gruyères was founded around 400 BC by Gruerius, a Vandal king, who saw a crane (French: grue) flying across the evening sky and chose to build his town on that spot. The coat of arms depicts a silver crane on a red field. No historical record confirms Gruerius's existence — the first documentary mention of "de Grueri" appears around 1138–1139 — but the legend elegantly ties together the town's name, heraldic identity, and sense of place.

The last count's secret

Count Michel's debts are mounting and creditors from Bern and Fribourg circle like hawks. A child discovers a hidden room in the castle containing the original crane banner, and must choose between saving the treasure or letting the old world pass — as five centuries of the Gruyère dynasty come to an end in bankruptcy.

Create This Story

The Counts of Gruyères ruled from their hilltop castle for almost five centuries, from the 12th century until 1554. The dynasty ended with Count Michel, who declared bankruptcy in 1554 after mounting debts. His properties were confiscated by Bern and Fribourg. Between 1555 and 1798, more than fifty bailiffs administered the region from the castle.

The cheese wheel that rolled away

A freshly pressed 35-kilogram wheel of Gruyère escapes from La Maison du Gruyère and rolls through the village, past the castle, down the hill. A child chases it, learning about cheesemaking at each stop — from the 400 liters of milk it takes to make one wheel to the months of aging that give it flavor.

Create This Story

Gruyère cheese has been produced in the region since at least 1115. Each wheel requires approximately 400 liters of milk, is pressed for about 20 hours, and weighs roughly 35 kg. La Maison du Gruyère, inaugurated in 1969 as Switzerland's first demonstration cheese dairy, produces 48 wheels per day. Gruyère received AOP status in 2011. Annual production across the region is approximately 29,000 tonnes.

Alien in the castle

A child enters the Giger Bar thinking it is a medieval dungeon, only to discover the biomechanical world of HR Giger inside — double arches of vertebrae on the ceiling, skeletal chairs, and the feeling of sitting inside an alien organism. The child must figure out how the strangest art museum in Switzerland ended up in the most charming village in the Pre-Alps.

Create This Story

HR Giger (1940–2014), born in Chur, designed the Xenomorph creature for Ridley Scott's Alien (1979), winning the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects in 1980. After falling in love with Gruyères, he purchased the Château Saint-Germain in 1997; the museum opened on June 21, 1998. The Giger Bar, opened April 12, 2003, was designed entirely by Giger — its interior of skeletal arches resembles being inside a fossilized creature, creating a surreal contrast with the medieval village.

The golden bread of Bénichon

At the harvest festival, a child learns to bake cuchaule — the golden saffron bread that is the queen of the Bénichon feast — with a mysterious old woman who claims the saffron was a gift from the crane on the coat of arms. Each ingredient tells the story of a season in the Gruyère hills.

Create This Story

Cuchaule is a golden saffron-infused brioche bread, first mentioned in 1558 and recognized with AOP status since 2018 — the queen of the Bénichon feast. The Bénichon is a centuries-old harvest festival unique to the canton of Fribourg, celebrated in the Gruyère region on the second Sunday of October. The traditional menu includes cuchaule, moutarde de Bénichon, poire à botzi, lamb stew, meringues with double cream, and bricelets.

The cows come home

A shy child joins the armaillis for the désalpe from the high pastures above Charmey. The lead cow's enormous bell has a magical tone that makes everyone who hears it dance. As the flower-crowned herd descends through autumn mist, the child discovers the ancient rhythm of transhumance that has shaped Gruyère life for centuries.

Create This Story

The désalpe is the annual autumn descent of cattle from Alpine summer pastures. The most famous in the Gruyère region takes place in Charmey every fourth Saturday of September. Cows wear enormous bells and floral headdresses; herders called armaillis wear the traditional bredzon (dark blue smock). Alphorn players, flag throwers, and brass bands accompany the procession. The milk from these cows, fed on rich Alpine grasses, is used to make Gruyère AOP cheese.

Corot's lost painting

In 1852, painter Jean-Baptiste Camille Corot left an unfinished landscape medallion hidden in the castle walls during his summer stay. A child follows clues through the Salle Corot to find the missing work, discovering the story of the artists' colony that transformed a bankrupt count's fortress into a temple of art.

Create This Story

In 1849, the Bovy brothers and the Balland family purchased the Château de Gruyères and established an artists' colony. Beginning in 1850, painter Daniel Bovy invited friends including the celebrated French painter Jean-Baptiste Camille Corot, who resided at the castle in June 1852 and 1857, painting four landscape medallions on the wood panelling. The room still bears his name. The castle also houses three capes of the Order of the Golden Fleece, captured at the Battle of Morat in 1476.

The four museums mystery

A treasure hunt linking clues across all four Gruyères museums: a medieval cloak in the castle, an alien sculpture in the Giger Museum, a Tibetan prayer bell in the Tibet Museum, and a cheese mold at La Maison du Gruyère. Together the clues reveal the crane's secret — and the reason this tiny village holds so many worlds.

Create This Story

Gruyères — a village of just 2,282 inhabitants — houses four museums within walking distance: the Château de Gruyères (cantonal museum since 1939), the HR Giger Museum (opened 1998), the Tibet Museum (Fondation Alain Bordier, opened 2009, with approximately 300 Buddhist art objects from the 6th–18th century), and La Maison du Gruyère (the demonstration cheese dairy, welcoming over 670,000 guests in 2019). The village is essentially a single cobblestone lane enclosed within ancient fortification walls.
